I don't either, and I'll be honest, when I looked at this my 1st time, most models I could run were quite ... retarded...tool or agentic workflow were not even feasible things, it would forget or not call it correctly.They could do basic text rewriting, search and information ... but they were really badThen due to the improvement models even lower than 10Billion parameters , if given access to websearch, could give decent answers and be used as Chat models.This was great , but the tok/s speed was not great in my little war machine, even so, worth it to explore a field like this.Most of the time , going over VRAM would mean sub 2tok/s speeds.With improvements to quantization and expecially MoE , we can now run offload models ( VRAM+RAM ) without going to abysmal speeds.

Right now (06/2026) I'm testing and using on my system ( 8gb VRAM+ 32GB RAM) Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-APEX-MTP-I-Compact.gguf

Gemma4-26B-A4B-QAT-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Balanced-Q4_K_M.gguf I'm sure this list will be outdated very soon.
I run llama.cpp locally with these settings .I get around 55-65tok/s in code.It exposes the API in my pc ( localhost ) port 8080
Currently I have these docker containers : Pewdiepie Odysseus ( comes with seaxng ) Opencode Pi Librechat
Cloudflare tunel to "expose" my AI/Harness to the internet Or I just use vs.code+ pluggin to connect to it.
I load the harness I want, all my harnesses ( unless it's a vs.code extension ) are running using docker ( WSL2 )
I load the docker container , and I setup the api endpoint ( usually it's already done )
Also I have some extra info on docker "maintenance" , here For an example of a docker container , here's my dockerfile.opencode :# Stage 1: Build snip binary FROM golang:alpine AS builder RUN go install github.com/edouard-claude/snip/cmd/snip@latest || \ go install github.com/VincentHardouin/opencode-snip/cmd/snip@latest # Stage 2: Build codebase-memory-mcp from source (Alpine/musl compatible) FROM alpine:latest AS cbm-builder RUN apk add --no-cache \ git \ gcc \ g++ \ make \ musl-dev \ zlib-dev \ bash RUN cd /tmp && \ git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/DeusData/codebase-memory-mcp.git && \ cd codebase-memory-mcp && \ ./scripts/build.sh # Stage 3: Final image FROM ghcr.io/anomalyco/opencode:latest RUN apk add --no-cache \ git \ bash \ curl \ wget \ nodejs \ npm \ python3 \ py3-pip \ ripgrep \ zlib # FIX: Bypass peer dependency conflict (upstream @opentui/core version mismatch) RUN npm install -g @tarquinen/opencode-dcp --legacy-peer-deps # Copy from /go/bin (not /root/go/bin) COPY --from=builder /go/bin/snip /usr/local/bin/opencode-snip RUN ln -s /usr/local/bin/opencode-snip /usr/local/bin/snip # Copy musl-built codebase-memory-mcp binary COPY --from=cbm-builder /tmp/codebase-memory-mcp/build/c/codebase-memory-mcp /usr/local/bin/codebase-memory-mcp RUN chmod +x /usr/local/bin/codebase-memory-mcp ENV PATH="/usr/local/bin:$PATH"
This will make a linux image to build every tool needed ( even a memory Mpc server ) , then copy it to another linux image ( final one ) and sets up OpenCode and extra tools for it to work ( bash , git , zlib , npm , wget )I just go to the folder where it is and run "docker build -t opencode"For example if we wanted it to use gcc , we can just ADD gcc to the list and it will be included in the final image.
Then I load OpenCode with a comand like this : wt.exe docker run -it --rm --name opencode ^ -v "D:\AI projects\opencode:/workspace" ^ -v "D:\AI projects\opencode-config:/root/.config/opencode" ^ -v "D:\AI projects\opencode-data:/root/.local/share/opencode" ^ -v "D:\AI projects\opencode-cbm-cache:/root/.cache/codebase-memory-mcp" ^ -w /workspace ^ opencode-full I use windows terminal ( wt.exe ) cause it looks better and handles mouse/keyboard better.Then I'm "binding" folders meaning that for example : "D:\AI projects\opencode-config" the windows folder will be in the linux image : "/root/.config/opencode" So I can go to the "D:\AI projects\opencode-config" folder and just edit the configuration."D:\AI projects\opencode" is binded to "/workspace"
meaning I place my files in "D:\AI projects\opencode" and now that docker will see them and interact with them.
the --rm here forces everything to dissapear ( except the binded folders ) and next OpenCodeexecution is "fresh" from the Image.the --name is just for me to easily find it.
This means opencode CAN't mess with anything other than those folders.

1-harness/what-is-an-harness.html.title: What is an harness
1-harness/what-is-an-harness.html.description:
A harness is the program that sits between you and the model : it takes your input, builds the actual prompt the model sees, sends it off for inference, and does something with the model's response.If you've only used something like ChatGPT or Claude's chat interface, this distinction is easy to miss, because the harness is invisible, the chat app is the harness, quietly doing a lot of work you never see: It adds a system prompt you never typed, telling the model how to behave It formats your message into whatever chat template the model expects (special tokens marking who said what) It keeps track of the conversation history and re-sends the relevant parts every time, since the model itself has no memory between requests If you attach a file or it does a web search, the harness fetches that content and stuffs it into context before the model ever sees your question If the AI can use tools (search the web, run code, check your calendar), the harness is the thing that actually executes those tool calls and feeds the results back in the model itself can't do anything, it can only say what it wants to do. When you're running a model through something like Claude or ChatGPT, all of this is baked in and tuned by the company that built it. But ... The moment you run a model locally, you become responsible for choosing (or building) the harness and different harnesses are built for very different jobs:
A General Chat UI (Open WebUI, LM Studio's chat) just handles conversation history and formatting
A Coding assistant (like an IDE plugin) adds your open files and project structure into context automatically
An Agentic Coding harness (OpenCode, Claude Code-style tools) goes much further it can read/write files, run terminal commands, call APIs, and loop the model through multiple steps on its own, deciding what to do with each result before deciding what to do next
An Agentic harness is the same as an Agentic Coding harness but focused on other things than coding , it might just check your email , check the news, stocks , and make reports on it every 6h . Same underlying model, wildly different behavior, because the harness controls what the model can see and what it's allowed to do with what it says.
This is also why a model that's great at chat can feel clumsy in an agentic harness, or vice versa: the harness has to match the model's actual strengths (tool-calling format, context handling, etc.) together with the System Prompts and the Main Prompt .

2-system-prompts-and-tools/agents-&-agen.title: Agents & Agentic Behavior
2-system-prompts-and-tools/agents-&-agen.description:
I have a list of places to get agents and skills.An agent is a runtime controller that wraps your model and gives it structured abilities: Planning : break tasks into steps
Tool use : call functions, MCP servers, APIs Observation loops : read tool output and continue
State : keep track of goals, memory, context Autonomy : decide what to do next Agents are not the model, Agents are not the prompt. Agents are the logic layer that turns a model into a worker.Agents unlock capabilities that raw LLMs cannot do: Write code → run code → fix error Search the web → extract data → summarize Read files → modify files → commit to Git Query databases → transform → export Plan multi-step tasks → execute → refine Without agents, your model is just a text generator in a single "GO"...Think of an Agent as a structure , where the AI plans , executes and tests and has a "loop" until it reaches the desired goal These files define the agent’s identity and behavior, including: its role its capabilities
its tools (MCP servers, function calls) its planning strategy
its Memory Usage its workflow rules its safety constraints Think of them as the “configuration + personality + operating manual” for an agent.They tell the agent how to act, not what to think.# Researcher Agent ## Role You are a Research Agent. Your purpose is to gather information, verify it across multiple sources, extract relevant details, and produce structured, accurate summaries. You specialize in technical research, documentation analysis, and multi‑source synthesis. ## Capabilities - Perform web searches - Fetch and extract content from URLs - Compare and cross‑verify information - Produce structured reports and summaries - Cite sources when possible - Store notes and findings in files - Maintain a research log using memory tools ## Tools - brave_search - fetch - read_file - write_file - memory_store - memory_retrieve ## Planning Strategy 1. Clarify the research question. 2. Generate a search plan (queries + target sources). 3. Fetch and extract content. 4. Compare findings across multiple sources. 5. Summarize results with citations. 6. Store notes for future reference. ## Workflow Rules - Always verify information using at least two sources. - Prefer primary sources over secondary commentary. - If uncertain, explicitly state uncertainty. - Keep summaries concise unless asked otherwise. ## Safety - Do not fetch unsafe or suspicious URLs. - Do not store sensitive data without explicit user approval. # Planner Agent ## Role You are a Planning Agent. Your job is to break complex tasks into actionable steps, create workflows, evaluate dependencies, and produce clear execution plans. You specialize in decomposition, sequencing, and strategy. ## Capabilities - Multi-step planning - Workflow generation - Task decomposition - Timeline estimation - Dependency mapping - Saving plans to files ## Tools - write_file - read_file - memory_store - memory_retrieve ## Planning Strategy 1. Understand the user’s goal. 2. Break the goal into logical phases. 3. Decompose phases into actionable tasks. 4. Identify dependencies and potential blockers. 5. Produce a step-by-step plan. 6. Optionally create timelines or structured workflow documents. ## Workflow Rules - Plans must be realistic and actionable. - Avoid unnecessary complexity. - Ask for clarification if the goal is ambiguous. - Provide alternatives when appropriate. ## Safety - Do not plan harmful or illegal activities. - Do not assume user intent; ask when unclear. # Coder Agent ## Role You are a Coding Agent. Your job is to read, write, fix, refactor, and execute code. You specialize in debugging, architecture, iterative improvement, and repository analysis. ## Capabilities - Read and analyze code - Write new code - Refactor existing code - Run code and inspect output - Debug errors and propose fixes - Work with Git repositories - Document code and changes ## Tools - read_file - write_file - execute_code - git_diff - git_status - fetch # for API documentation or external references ## Planning Strategy 1. Read relevant files to understand the problem. 2. Propose a fix or implementation plan. 3. Modify or write code as needed. 4. Execute code or tests to validate changes. 5. Iterate until stable and error-free. 6. Document changes or create commits when appropriate. ## Workflow Rules - Never overwrite files without explicit confirmation. - Always explain code changes clearly. - If execution fails, analyze the error and retry. - Keep commits small, meaningful, and well-documented. ## Safety - Do not run destructive commands. - Do not modify files outside the designated workspace. Creating a plan like : Implement X feature Gather info on X feature Implement it Test it
Agents decide when to call tools. Example : fetch(url) read_file(path) sql_query(query) git_diff(commit) maps_directions(origin, destination)
Tools come from MCP servers or local Harness tools.After a tool runs, the agent reads the output and continues/adjusts.for example : Tool output: "File not found" Agent: "Try alternative path"
Agents store and retrieve long-term context.Example: store project goals remember user preferences track previous steps Agents decide what to do next without user input.Example: “I need to fetch more data.” “I should run the code to test it.” “I will rewrite the function to fix the error.”

2-system-prompts-and-tools/mcp-servers.h.title: MCP servers
2-system-prompts-and-tools/mcp-servers.h.description:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are programs that expose capabilities to an AI model through a standardized interface. They act as the “tool layer” of an Agentic system, the part that lets the model do things instead of just generating text.
MCP servers are not part of the model, they are not part of the system prompt, and are not a part of the Harness. They provide three types of capabilities: Tools : active operations the model can call (write files, query APIs, search GitHub). Resources : passive read-only data (file contents, database schemas, calendars). Application Prompts : prebuilt instruction templates for workflows (summarize meetings, plan trips). Think of them like "servers" that connect to your AI and give them tools and/or knowledge on how to run the tools.This should make you understand what is an MPC server ... there is an unrealengine mcp , a sqlmcp , a lot of mcp servers are out there , and it's a great way to give your model tools.
They can be Local , to give access to a filesystem , or a specific program.There is a "blender-mcp server" , and connecting your AI to it , lets it "control blender" trough the mcp server.There are online MCP servers , here's some examples ( they need an API key )Brave MCP : wss://mcp.brave.com/search Gives you search on your model and teaches it how to use it. Google maps : wss://mcp.googleapis.com/maps Now your AI knows how to read maps , give directions

2-system-prompts-and-tools/prompt-engine.title: Prompt Engineering
2-system-prompts-and-tools/prompt-engine.description:
Prompt engineering is the practice of designing inputs that reliably produce the outputs you want from an LLM. It’s not about “magic words”.It’s about structure, constraints, and clarity. Good prompts make models predictable, controllable, and efficient.You also want to give it all relevant information and context , when asking in OpenCode about your code , OpenCode WILL send that code to the AI , giving it context.On direct talks , we need to do it manually. Be explicit. Use structure. Use constraints. Avoid ambiguity. Prefer short prompts (as short as you can) over long ones. Use examples when formatting matters. Use output structuring for predictable results. Use agent‑style prompting for autonomous workflows. Test prompts with multiple models. Iterate.
Claude Prompt Guide
OpenAI Prompt guide
Promptingguide.ai
PromptHero
Awesome Prompt Engineering Here are some prompt ideas , you can use several , but gives a general idea.Define who the model is and what expertise it should emulate.Pattern: You are a <role>. You specialize in <domain>. Adopt the mindset and reasoning style of a professional. Example : "You are a senior software engineer specializing in debugging Python applications."Give explicit, direct instructions.Pattern: Your task is to <do X> Follow the instructions exactly. Do not add extra commentary. Example : "Your task is to rewrite the function for clarity and performance."Limit the output format or behavior.Pattern: Output only in <format>. No additional text. Keep the answer under <N> words. Example : "Output only valid JSON. No prose."Force structured thinking before answering.Pattern: Break the solution into steps. Explain reasoning briefly. Then provide the final answer. Example: "Think step‑by‑step before giving the final solution."Show examples to teach the model the desired pattern.Pattern: Example Input → Example Output pairs Then provide a new input Example: Example Input: “Sort: 3,1,2” Example Output: “1,2,3” Now sort: 9,4,7Tell the model when and how to use external capabilities.Pattern: If you need external data, call the appropriate tool. Explain why you are calling the tool. After receiving the result, evaluate it before continuing. Example: "If you need more information, call the fetch tool."Used in agent.md / claude.md definitions.Pattern: Follow a think → act → observe loop. After each action, evaluate the result. Stop when the goal is achieved. Ask for clarification if the objective is ambiguous. Example: "Think about the next best action before calling any tool."Prevent unwanted behavior.*Pattern: Do not perform actions outside the defined scope Ask for clarification if the request is unclear or unsafe. Never assume missing details. Example: "If the user request is ambiguous, ask for clarification."Tell the model how to behave when something goes wrong.Pattern: Identify the cause. Propose a fix. Retry the solution. Example: "If an error occurs, explain the cause and attempt a corrected version."Force predictable formatting.Example : Respond using this exact structure:## SUMMARY <one paragraph> ## DETAILS - <bullet point> - <bullet point> - <bullet point> ## OUTPUT <final answer in required format> ## CHECK - Requirements met: Yes/No - Constraints respected: Yes/No - Uncertainties: <list> Ask the model to generate multiple reasoning paths and choose the best one.Example: "Generate three different approaches. Select the most accurate one. Provide only the final answer."Prevent hallucinations.Example: "If you are unsure, say “I am not certain.” Do not invent facts. Ask for clarification when needed."

2-system-prompts-and-tools/system-prompt.title: System Prompt for Harness
2-system-prompts-and-tools/system-prompt.description:
A system‑prompt design must match the Harness it powers, because each harness expects a different style of reasoning, autonomy, and output discipline. A chat harness needs a light, conversational frame that emphasizes correctness, clarity, and uncertainty handling.
A coding harness needs strict rules: deterministic formatting, no hallucinated APIs, and minimal prose. An agentic coding harness requires a deeper framing that instructs the model to plan, break tasks into steps, verify assumptions, and use tools deliberately.
A full agentic harness needs the strongest framing of all explicit autonomy boundaries, confirmation rules, and a clear separation between planning and acting.
If the system prompt doesn’t match the harness, the model drifts: chat models start acting agentic, coding models start explaining instead of producing code, and agentic models skip planning or misuse tools. The system prompt is therefore the “behavioral contract” that aligns the model with the harness’s expectations.
An an example here's a system prompt for OpenCode ( OpenCode has it's own , so this isn't needed, it's just an example )You are an OpenCode assistant. Your only job is to produce **correct, runnable, minimal code** for the user. Always follow these rules: • Output **only code** unless the user explicitly asks for explanation. • Never invent APIs, functions, or libraries that do not exist. • Prefer standard libraries unless the user specifies otherwise. • If the request is ambiguous or impossible, state the limitation clearly. • Maintain deterministic formatting: no extra commentary, no mixed prose. • When multiple solutions exist, choose the simplest correct one. • If the user provides a codebase, follow its style and conventions exactly. • Do not modify unrelated parts of the code unless asked. • If you are unsure, ask for clarification instead of guessing.

2-system-prompts-and-tools/system-prompt.title: System Prompts
2-system-prompts-and-tools/system-prompt.description:
A system prompt is the foundational instruction that defines how an LLM should behave before any user message is ever seen. It sets the model’s role, constraints, tone, reasoning style, and allowed actions. Unlike normal prompts, the system prompt acts as a behavioral contract: it tells the model what it is, what it must prioritize, and what it must avoid. Everything the model does ( tool calling, reasoning depth, autonomy, formatting ) flows from this initial framing.In practice, a system prompt is the “operating rules” for a Harness.
A chat Harness uses it to enforce clarity and safety; a coding harness uses it to enforce determinism and strict formatting; an agentic harness uses it to define planning, tool usage, and autonomy boundaries. Because LLMs are highly sensitive to system‑level instructions, a well‑designed system prompt is the difference between a stable assistant and one that hallucinates, misuses tools, or behaves unpredictably.For a "normal web user" the system prompt is usually invisible.

2-system-prompts-and-tools/tool-and-func.title: Tool and function calling
2-system-prompts-and-tools/tool-and-func.description: Takeaway: Models differ dramatically in how they expose tools. Your system prompt must define the tool protocol, error behavior, and fallback strategy.Note : in llama.cpp you should use --jinja Note: Qwen models usually improve by using this : https://huggingface.co/froggeric/Qwen-Fixed-Chat-Templates/tree/main downloading the chat_template.jinja , and using --jinja --chat-template-file chat_template.jinja ^ JSON‑native models (Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek): Expect strict JSON schema. Fail silently if fields are missing or types mismatch. XML‑style tool callers (older OpenAI, some proprietary setups): Require wrapper tags like <tool> or <function_call>. Hybrid models: Accept JSON but require a wrapper (e.g., "tool": { ... }"). Your system prompt must explicitly define: Tool invocation rules (“Only call tools when explicitly instructed.”) Schema strictness (“Follow the exact JSON schema provided.”) Error handling (“If a tool call fails or schema is unclear, ask for clarification.”) Verification loop (“After receiving tool output, verify correctness before continuing.”)

3-models-info/context-&-kv-cache.html.title: Context & Kv Cache
3-models-info/context-&-kv-cache.html.description:
Context is what you ( or yourHarness ) send to the AI.
In a Basic task if you ask your Harness to "check my emails" , the Harness will send the emails in the prompt.If the AI needs to search a program's library to learn how it works, that documentation gets pulled in and added to the context too.
This matters because context isn't free every token in it costs processing time and (on local models especially) memory via the Kv Cache.
A Harness that dumps in more than it needs slows things down and can push relevant information further from the model's "attention," even before you hit the model's context limit.Do note that some models do have problems with context before their limit.You might see a model with max 256k context , that struggles after 120k context and gets "lost".When you reach the limit , the model will just bork, so most programs when reaching a limit will try to "compact" the Context and save only relevant thing.When a model processes text, each token gets converted into two internal representations inside every attention layer : a Key Value vector Key is usually more important.These are what the model uses to figure out how much attention each token should pay to every other token that came before it.Without a cache, the model would have to recompute the Key and Value vectors for the entire conversation from scratch on every single new token.The KV cache stores these vectors as they're generated, so each new token only needs its own K/V computed once, then reuses everything already cached from before.This is what makes autoregressive generation (one token at a time) fast.
But it comes at a cost: the cache grows with every token in your Context, and it lives in VRAM/RAM right alongside the model weights. Long context = a big KV cache = lots of memory needed
This is also why KV cache Quantization exists: you can compress the K and V vectors down from 16-bit to lower precision to fit longer context in the same VRAM, at some small cost to quality.Though I do not recommend going bellow Q8
3-models-info/context-&-kv-cache.html.sourcePath: AI/Organized info/3 Models Info/Context & Kv Cache.md

3-models-info/finetunes.html.title: Finetunes
3-models-info/finetunes.html.description: Finetunes are customized versions of a base model that have been trained further on a specific style, domain, or behavior. Instead of building a new model from scratch, a finetune takes an existing foundation model ( like Qwen, Llama, or DeepSeek )and “specializes” it using additional data. This extra training can shape how the model speaks, how it reasons or how it performs in coding tasks. Because the base model already understands language and structure, finetuning is far cheaper and faster than full training while still producing dramatic changes in personality and capability. Finetunes are popular in the local‑LLM community because they let creators build models tailored for specific use cases: expressive character chat, high‑precision coding, jailbreak‑resistant assistants, or stylistic personas like Qwopus or Ornith.Even though these finetunes may feel like entirely new models, they’re still built on top of the original architecture and inherit its strengths and limitations. The finetune simply reshapes how the model behaves, responds, and prioritizes information.As example of finetunes to the Qwen3.6 35B we have Qwopus and OrnithThere are also different quantization tricks.Some people also check the "locks" the AI has , and run it uncensored , removing said locks , it seems they are more prone to hallucinations , but when a model refuses to teach you how to "hack the memory of a game" , these finetunes might help ( but it seems most of the comunity uses uncensored builds for porn )

3-models-info/memory-usage.html.title: Memory Usage
3-models-info/memory-usage.html.description:
When you run a local model, your VRAM (or RAM, if offloading to CPU) gets split across a few distinct things, and it's worth knowing where it's all going: Model weights : the biggest fixed cost, determined by parameter count and Model Quantization level
Context & Kv Cache : grows with context length, separate from the weights Compute buffer / activations : temporary working memory the model needs during the actual forward pass, usually a smaller slice but not negligible
For example if you are planning to use a Dense model with 23Gb VRAM ( 24GB Vram but Os is using 1Gb ) and the model itself is 18Gb , this means you have 5Gb Extra VRAM for Context & Kv Cache. I would use 4Gb for Context in this scenario , so it has enough for compute buffer.
If your planning to use a MoE model then offloading isn't as bad , so your choosing what number of "experts" are loaded into VRAM , and what is offloaded.
The model total weight is "split" between VRAM+RAM , Kv Cache can be split ( but I recommend to keep it in VRAM for performance ).

3-models-info/moe-vs-dense.html.title: Moe Vs Dense
3-models-info/moe-vs-dense.html.description: Right now models are mostly either "DENSE" or "MoE" ( Mixture of Experts)A Dense model will always activate everything , meaning if it's divided into several gpus or gpu+RAM , you will struggle more. Dense models are usually better , at least at the time of writing this a 30B Dense of same family will beat a 35B MoE.Dense models are more predictable since the model activates every token for calculations. At least for me , it seems it's easier to finetune these models.Dense models need to fit the VRAM , or you will have really low output.MoE models use experts , meaning when you call it , it will get only the experts, meaning obviously having the model in gpu+cpu isn't has "damaging" for the speed.It has a "routing" part ( that routes to experts ) that from my experience very hard to finetune and often is easy to break ( undoubtedly due to my lack of knowledge ) It has the Experts , that are easier to finetune.Due to it's nature , it's usually faster than Dense.If you wanna run a model that uses VRAM+RAM use this.In lmstudio , you have settings to control the offloadIn llama.cpp we use --cpu-moe to control the offloadGemma 25B A4B, A4B means "4B parameters" are activated per token. The routing will route the request and use the "correct" experts.The 25B need to be active in RAM+VRAM , but each "call" will only use 4B.That's the whole tradeoff MoE is built around: you pay full memory cost for total capacity, but only pay compute cost for the active slice.
3-models-info/moe-vs-dense.html.sourcePath: AI/Organized info/3 Models Info/Moe Vs Dense.md

3-models-info/see-images-,-vision-,-mmpr.title: See images , vision  , mmproj
3-models-info/see-images-,-vision-,-mmpr.description: Vision models = the model itself has multimodal capability (can "see" images, not just read text).A vision-capable LLM isn't one single network, it's really two models bolted together: The vision encoder (usually a CLIP-style or SigLIP-style model) : takes a raw image, chops it into patches, and turns it into a sequence of embedding vectors. This part has never seen language, it just understands visual features. A projection layer : a small MLP that translates those vision embeddings into the same vector space the LLM's token embeddings live in, so the LLM can "read" them like it reads text tokens. The LLM itself : the language model you already know, which now also accepts these projected image-embedding "tokens" mixed into the sequence alongside text tokens. The mmproj file is what enables vision , I personally since I use it for coding I do not load it ( saving VRAM )This split exists partly for practical reasons: the vision tower is architecturally totally different from a transformer decoder (conv layers, patch embeddings, different attention pattern), so it made sense to keep it as its own loadable component rather than force-fit it into the same tensor format as the LLM.What this means practically: Downloading a vision model means grabbing two files, not one. If a repo only has the text .gguf and no mmproj-*.gguf, you can still run it as a text-only model, you just lose image input. So to load a model with vision you have to enable the flag in llama.cppLMStudio automatically loads mmproj , just delete the file if you don't wanna use it.
3-models-info/see-images-,-vision-,-mmpr.sourcePath: AI/Organized info/3 Models Info/See images , vision  , mmproj.md

4-running/api-management-&-routing.html.title: API Management & Routing
4-running/api-management-&-routing.html.description:
You might want to use a routing system , so it automatically changes from any local system to onlineAPI.I personally do this manually LiteLLM is an open source gateway. It exposes a single OpenAI compatible API endpoint that routes requests to over 100 different LLM providers. These providers include OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Together AI, and local servers like llama.cpp.
You can run LiteLLM as a lightweight local proxy or as a docker container. Its memory footprint is small, roughly 65 MB, and the added latency per request is under 1 millisecond.
note : LiteLLM has built in caching with backends for memory, disk, and Redis. It supports semantic caching when using Redis or Valkey as the vector store. You can run Valkey in a local docker container to enable this without any cloud dependency.This is "kinda" illegal , but there is a system that "hijacks" the anti-gravity models to other CLIs It also can use several accounts and jump from one to another as soon as the limits reach.
Also you can run it as a docker containerDO NOT USE YOUR MAIN GOOGLE ACCOUNT WITH THIS

4-running/hosted‑but‑self‑run-models.htm.title: Hosted‑but‑self‑run models
4-running/hosted‑but‑self‑run-models.htm.description:
I do this sometimes , This means you control a server , and you install the model in it.This means you still control the model and it's data , and it's all "clear" as soon as you disable the server. I do use some "free options" ( but if you have money you can just buy/rent a server ) Kaggle https://www.kaggle.com/
Google colab : https://colab.research.google.com/ Kaggle gives you a 2xT4 ( 2x16GB + 32-50 RAM ) , I think about 20h per week Colab gives you a 1xT4 ( 16Gb +12 RAM ) , I really dunno the limit.This is what I recommend for setting them up :
You make a model selection cell with extra parameters : Select model ( and proper model location to download from hugging face )
Context limit ubatch+batch ( for tuning speed ) mmap on/off ( for tuning speed )
KV cache Quantization options MTP DRAFT and max draft limit ( speed tunning ) Jinja template on or off ( for coding ) Threads ( for tuning )
Sampling Parameters API key (security) Then those values are passed to cell2 , cell 2 downloads llama.cpp , sets up everything uses the cell1 info to download model and run llama.cppCell3 should be a proxy to expose it outside , there are some ways to do this : ngrok Cloudflare tunels zgrok Nport TunnelAPI instatunnel Loophole Make it output the API endpoint + key , so you can easily setup. some of these offer random endpoints , others have custom ( always the same )
Then just connect your Harness to the API endpoint you created.I'm sure any online model will help you setting up this idea quickly.

4-running/local.html.title: Local
4-running/local.html.description:
I personally recommend to use a simple "loader" like LM studio on windows to test/search models and then use llama.cpp to test actual performance , if your in linux just go straight to llama.cppTest some models , if your goal is to have a chat assistant , a coding assistant, or an Agentic Coding make some tests to test it's performance, both in quality of answer and speed.Right now as I'm writing this , for my system ( 8gb VRAM + 32GB VRAM ) it's hard to beat Qwen3.6 35B MoE or it's finetunes ( Qwopus , ornith , etc ) for my use cases.
For me personally I can get arround 50-60tok/s with qwen3.6 35B with 128k context , I find it very acceptable due to my hardware constrains.I personally have this setup:
llama.cpp running native with these settings
docker container running:
OpenCode
pi
Odyseeus ( pewdiepie )
LibreChat I recommend to most local users to use MoE models, since most of us aren't swimming in 64GB VRAM gpus .... ( sadly )
Look at how much RAM+VRAM you have to spare , how much context you want , and pick your models.
Grab llama.cpp and/or LM studio from the Downloads and start testing
I left an example llama.cpp launch hereCheck new releases , but I would recomend using lmStudio to get and run the models
it's GUI is quite easy to check how much VRAM it will use with context target.After you find a good candidate , move to llama.cpp , run it with low context ( 4k ) and check VRAM usage.
Then increase context untill your happy and keep an eye VRAM.
mess with llama.cpp --cpu-moe untill you used most of the VRAM ( leave some room )
Then decide if you want to sacrifice context size or context quantization ) to put "more" into the GPU , or vice versa.
here you can only mess with context quants and model quants , since offloading = VERY LOW speeds.
More info here
When using any sort of Harness that allows to run code/script ( Odysseus , OpenCode , etc ) I recommend using docker to have a "virtual space" for the AI
This avoid issues , since the giving the AI access to run code/shell in your main OS **CAN RUIN YOUR SYSTEM** .
So if OpenCode does something really bad , it just ruins it's docker container.

4-running/the-software-stack.html.title: The Software Stack
4-running/the-software-stack.html.description:
{Model itself} ----API---->{Harness}So installing a Harness you can connect to Online API List or a Local run AI and use it.
Fell free to also use other docker containers , for example , we can install "seaXNG" as a docker container.{Model itself running locally} ---API--->{Docker with Harness}--->{Docker SeaXNG}

4-running/virtualized-,-isolated-runtime.title: Virtualized , Isolated Runtime Environments
4-running/virtualized-,-isolated-runtime.description:
(Docker, Containers, Sandboxes)Running LLMs inside a virtualized environment means the model doesn’t touch your main OS directly. Instead, it runs inside a controlled “box” with its own filesystem, dependencies, ports, and resource limits. This is ideal for experimentation, safety, reproducibility, and keeping your main machine clean. Isolation : the AI can’t modify your main OS, files, or environment. Reproducibility : same environment everywhere: laptop, server, cloud. Clean dependencies : CUDA, Python, llama.cpp versions stay inside the container. Easy resets : break something? docker rm and start fresh. Security : models/tools run in a sandbox, not your host OS. Portability : move your entire AI stack to another machine in seconds. Share the host kernel Lightweight Near‑zero overhead Perfect for LLM runtimes (llama.cpp, OpenWebUI, MCP servers) Full OS inside another OS Heavy GPU passthrough is painful For LLMs: containers are much easierI'll ignore VMs since I'm not using them.
Download Massive ecosystem : almost every LLM tool ships an official image (ollama/ollama, ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui, ghcr.io/mcp-searxng/searxng)
docker-compose lets you define your whole stack ([Program and Sources#Programs to "run" LLMs|llama.cpp]] + OpenWebUI + SearXNG + MCP servers) as one file, one up command GPU passthrough is a solved problem via NVIDIA Container Toolkit not painful like VM passthrough In linux you have to install "NVIDIA Container Toolkit" for passtrough to work Ignore this if your on windowsAn image is the read-only template, the filesystem snapshot a container is built from (OS layer + installed packages + your app code). Every docker pull or docker build downloads/creates one.docker images # list all images with size docker image ls -a # include intermediate/dangling ones Images are built in layers, each RUN/COPY/ADD line in a Dockerfile creates a new layer, cached and reused across builds. This is why a 2GB CUDA base image only needs downloading once even if you build 10 different llama.cpp variants on top of it they "share" the base layers.What eats space here: pulling the same image tagged differently (llama.cpp:server-cuda and llama.cpp:full-cuda share almost nothing if built from different Dockerfiles), and old versions you never removed after pulling updates.A container is a running (or stopped) instance of an image, plus a thin writable layer on top for any runtime changes (logs, temp files, anything written inside the container that isn't in a volume).bashdocker ps -a # all containers, including stopped docker container ls -s # -s shows size (base image size + writable layer) What eats space here: stopped containers you forgot about. docker run without --rm leaves the container (and its writable layer) sitting on disk even after it exits. If you're iterating on a config and re-running the same image repeatedly without --rm, you accumulate dozens of dead containers, each with their own small-but-nonzero writable layer.You can run containers as "static" or run them as -rm , this way as soon as you close the window , that container is gonne ( but the image remains )Persistent data that survives container deletion, this is where your actual data lives (Open WebUI's chat history, a model cache, a database). Volumes are managed by Docker and stored under /var/lib/docker/volumes/ on Linux (or the WSL2 virtual disk on Windows).docker volume ls docker volume inspect open-webui-data # shows the actual host path There are two kinds of volumes using: Named volumes (d:\AIstuff\open-webui-data:/app/backend/data) : Docker-managed, lives in Docker's storage area, survives docker rm of the container. Since the data is actually in `d:\AIstuff\open-webui-data´ and will be there again when loaded. Bind mounts (./models:/models) : just a folder on your host mapped in directly. Not really "Docker space" at all, it's your own filesystem, so it doesn't get cleaned up by any docker prune command, you manage it like any other folder. What eats space here: named volumes for containers you deleted long ago. Deleting a container does not delete its named volumes by default,they're orphaned and just sit there. This is one of the most common sources of silent disk bloat in a Docker setup that's been running for months.Separate from images : this is the intermediate layer cache Docker keeps around to speed up rebuilds (your opencode-full custom image, for instance).docker builder du # size of the build cache specifically This can balloon fast if you're iterating on a Dockerfile a lot, every failed/changed layer during development gets cached "just in case," and it's easy to lose track of gigabytes here since it's not visible in docker images.Usually it's the safest to "prune"docker system df # summary: images, containers, volumes, build cache sizes docker system df -v # verbose, per-item breakdown This is the single most useful command , run it first before cleaning anything, so you know where the space is actually going instead of guessing.To clean the build cache ( no problem doing this , it will just make next installation slower ) docker builder prune"dangling images" are images that aren't connected , sometimes when you update a docker it will leave "old versions" you can check by doing this docker images -f "dangling=true" and docker image prune to clean themBe very careful since a lot of cleanup commands will nuke your non running volumes.Always backup your install methods , so you can just re-install them, when eventually you "prune" away a used image/volume
If you mount your Volumes correctly , you will keep the data.docker container prune # removes ALL stopped containers docker image prune # removes dangling (untagged) images only docker image prune -a # removes ALL images not used by a running container docker volume prune # removes volumes not referenced by ANY container (careful , checks name attachment, not usage) docker builder prune # clears build cache docker system prune -a --volumes # nuclear option: all of the above at once On Windows, Docker Desktop's WSL2 backend stores everything inside a single growing virtual disk file (ext4.vhdx). Even after you prune inside Docker, that .vhdx file doesn't shrink automatically, Docker frees the internal space but Windows doesn't reclaim it from the physical disk. You have to manually compact it:wsl --shutdown diskpart Inside diskpart:select vdisk file="C:\Users\<you>\AppData\Local\Docker\wsl\data\ext4.vhdx" attach vdisk readonly compact vdisk detach vdisk exit Get your actual vhdx file location ( docker desktop )docker exec -it opencode ash or docker exec -it opencode bash
In this case I'm "opening" the OpenCode container and running ash/bash , change the name for your case.docker run -d --name cf-3333 cloudflare/cloudflared:latest tunnel --url http://host.docker.internal:3333An example simple batch script would be:@echo off echo Starting Cloudflare tunnels... docker run -d --name cf-3000 cloudflare/cloudflared:latest tunnel --url http://host.docker.internal:3000 docker run -d --name cf-8000 cloudflare/cloudflared:latest tunnel --url http://host.docker.internal:8000 echo. echo Tunnels started. Getting URLs... timeout /t 3 >nul echo. echo Port 3000: docker logs cf-3000 | findstr trycloudflare.com echo. echo Port 8000: docker logs cf-8000 | findstr trycloudflare.com echo. echo Done. Stop them in Docker Desktop when done. pause a "more complex" batch script that you can add more ports:@echo off setlocal enabledelayedexpansion set PORTS=3030 3333 for %%P in (%PORTS%) do ( set NAME=cf-%%P docker stop cf-%%P >nul 2>&1 docker run -d --rm --name cf-%%P cloudflare/cloudflared:latest tunnel --url http://host.docker.internal:%%P ) echo. echo A aguardar URLs do Cloudflare... timeout /t 3 /nobreak >nul for %%P in (%PORTS%) do ( set URL= for /l %%I in (1,1,15) do ( if not defined URL_%%P ( for /f "tokens=*" %%L in ('docker logs cf-%%P 2^>^&1') do ( echo %%L | findstr /r "https://[a-zA-Z0-9-]*\.trycloudflare\.com" >nul 2>&1 if !errorlevel! == 0 ( for /f "tokens=*" %%U in ('echo %%L ^| findstr /o /r "https://[a-zA-Z0-9-]*\.trycloudflare\.com"') do ( set URL_%%P=%%L ) ) ) ) if not defined URL_%%P timeout /t 1 /nobreak >nul ) if defined URL_%%P ( echo cf-%%P -^> !URL_%%P! ) else ( echo cf-%%P -^> URL nao encontrado (verifica: docker logs cf-%%P) ) ) pause these will create a docker that just routs one or more ports and give you a cloudflare hostname exposing it to the internet.

5-performance/quantizations/kv-cache-qua.title: KV cache Quantization
5-performance/quantizations/kv-cache-qua.description:
KV cache can be quantized , uses less memory for context , usually you start noticing on "higher" levels of context.K "is more important" than V
If a model gets lost in stuff @ 150k context with F16 , making the KV cache Q8 I would guess it would start to get "confused" at 120-130ishIn llama you use --cache-type-k / --cache-type-v. Requires -fa on (flash attention) for turbo types, or you eat a dequant penalty every step.Vector-quantized via "WHT + Lloyd-Max codebooks", calibration-free (no imatrix-style pre-pass needed). Numbers below are from real fork benchmarks (Qwen3-14B Q4_K_M, RX 9070 XT):Asymmetric best practice (per fork docs): K tolerates compression far worse than V. Something like ... --cache-type-k q8_0 --cache-type-v turbo4 rather than compressing both sides equallyRight now as I type this , turboquant is quite new.Turbo seems quite decent, but personally I wouldn't go bellow Q8 ( and if I had to I would recomend only Vcache turbo4 )

5-performance/quantizations/model-quanti.title: Model Quantization
5-performance/quantizations/model-quanti.description: I think most models start as 16-bit floats (BF16/FP16)every single "token" uses 4 bytes , by quantizing (simplifying here) we're making them use 8,6,4,2 bits.The model becomes smaller , but obviously with lossesSizes/RAM include ~10–15% compute buffer overhead. Does not include KV cache size that separately based on context length and --cache-type-k/v.using Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (~36B params)Controls how many tensors get bumped to a higher bit-width than the base quant level not a different algorithm. S (small): base bit-width almost everywhere , smallest file, most compression M (medium): a few sensitive tensors (some attention/output layers) raised one level , modest size increase, real accuracy gain L (large): more tensors raised, biggest of the three, closest to the tier above, sometimes overlaps it in practice Only Q2_K, Q3_K, Q4_K, Q5_K get S/M/L variants in stock llama.cpp. Q6_K and Q8_0 ship as single fixed formats with no split.At a size similar to Q4_K_S, it tends to preserve accuracy slightly better and decodes faster on CPU There is also many specific ways to "quantize" that aren't mentioned here, and probably many more to come. Quality-loss column is general guess Seems on Q8 it doesn't help , but Q6 and bellow it really helps.When model was quantized it was using an importance matrix: the quantizer ran calibration data through the full-precision model first to learn which weights matter most, then protected those during quantizationAfaik it's simillar to APEX , in training you check what weights in the model actually move the output, and give more "value" to those.The catch: quality depends on what calibration dataset was used (code-heavy calibration vs general text calibration will bias the model's strengths slightly)usually at the same size, imatrix beats static.APEX = "Adaptive Precision for EXpert Models" , it's specifically made for MoE models , since it uses different quants to different experts , it's nomenclature changes There are other things like this.

headers.heading: S / M / L suffix , what it means

5-performance/benchmarks-and-efficiency..title: Benchmarks and efficiency
5-performance/benchmarks-and-efficiency..description:
Published benchmark scores are the least reliable part of any model card.Contamination (test sets leaking into training data), cherry-picked prompting templates, and benchmark-specific fine-tuning ("benchmaxxing") mean a model can top a leaderboard and still feel worse than a lower-scoring model in your actual workflow. Treat every benchmark number as a hypothesis to verify, not a conclusion.For your coding specifically, the benchmark that actually matters is SWE-bench Verified (real GitHub issues, real repo context, requires actual patch generation + test passing) These matter more than MMLU/HumanEval (agentic coding stack, OpenCode/Cline, real repo work).
SWE-bench Verified is your best single proxy for "will this model be a good daily driver for Agentic Coding"
Watch for score inflation via Harness tuning. Some labs report SWE-bench numbers using heavily engineered scaffolds (multiple attempts, self-verification loops, custom retrieval) that don't match what you'll get running raw through OpenCode. Always check whether the number is "model alone" or "model + elaborate Harness."
Edit-format compliance (Aider-style) is a separate skill from Coding ability. A model can reason through the right fix and still fail because it can't emit a diff your tool can parse, this is the same failure class as an Agentic JSON tool-call issue, just for code edits instead of function calls.
None of these measure long-loop degradation well except METR-style benchmarks. SWE-bench is single-shot-ish (one issue, one patch). IF your multi-critic Harness runs many turns, that's a different reliability axis almost no public benchmark captures , which is why your own eval set still matters more than any leaderboard.
A few points of difference is noise, not signal. 73.4% vs 72% on SWE-bench Verified is within run-to-run variance territory for many Harnesses. Don't chase small deltas.
LLM-as-judge benchmarks (MT-Bench, Arena-Hard) measure "sounds good to another LLM," not correctness. They correlate reasonably with human preference for chat, but are weak signals for Coding/Agentic/factual tasks.
Static benchmarks miss something: tool-call JSON reliability, instruction adherence over long Agentic loops, context handling at your working context length, tokens/sec at your hardware (none of which MMLU/HumanEval touch at all). Two models with identical benchmark scores can differ hugely in practice Treat leaderboards as a shortlist filter, not a ranking. Use them to cut a field of 50 models down to 5
Rule of thumb: benchmarks tell you what's plausible , use lmarena or make your own tests.

5-performance/draft-dflash-mtp-future-pr.title: DRAFT Dflash MTP future prediction
5-performance/draft-dflash-mtp-future-pr.description:
A Draft system explained simply is when you have something that writes fast, and trying to "guess", then if it guesses correctly the AI simply writes faster.As a decent way to see it , imagine an AI with 30B parameters, and now a small AI attached with 1B parameters, and we let the second AI "Try to guess" the next 4 words.If we get an ok acceptance rate ( 0.90 , 0.80 , 0.70 , 0.60 ) this would give us a LOT of extra speed.It really comes handy in coding, since it's easy "looking back" to guess what we'r doing, a lot of repetition and syntax.To simplify all of these "predicts" are measured by "acceptance rate" , you usually loose performance, but then gain it, when the "draft model" accurately predicts tokens in front.We have to balance time spent predicting a maxdraft of 16 would create a LOT of negatives , thus probably lowering our tok/s by a LOT.We run a lighter model , same family to try to predict the future tokens.Instead of a separate model, the target model itself has extra lightweight prediction heads trained to predict tokens sharing the same backbone/hidden states. This is baked into the architecture at training time (DeepSeek-V3 popularized this). Because it's self-contained, there's no separate draft model to manage, and acceptance rates tend to be higher since it's the same model's "opinion" about future tokens, just from a "shallower" head.Quite "new" draft-dflash uses a small draft model that reads the target model's hidden states to predict the next tokens, reaching higher acceptance than a standalone draft model.I haven't tested this much since it's not implemented into llama.cpp ( should be VERY SOON )Recently released from deepseek , currently only for deepseek V4 ( when you read this maybe it's out =)) )ngram-mod is here cause I dunno where to put it , you can use it in llama.cpp , and it basically tries to predict the next 32 or 64 tokens, sometimes it gives stalls, so test with your system. --spec-type ngram-mod ^
--spec-ngram-mod-n-max 32 ^

5-performance/llama.cpp-settings.html.title: llama.cpp Settings
5-performance/llama.cpp-settings.html.description:
Here's an example launch configuration for llama.cpp that I use in a 8gb VRAM gpu + 32Gb RAM.set LIMEAIMODEL=D:\Models\mudler\Qwopus3.6-35B-A3B-v1-APEX-MTP-GGUF\Qwopus3.6-35B-A3B-v1-APEX-MTP-I-Compact.gguf set LIMEAIAPI_KEY=Apikey-12345 set LIMEAICTX=131072 wt.exe F:\AIstuff\llama.cpp\llama-server.exe -m "%LIMEAIMODEL_PATH%" ^ --host 127.0.0.1 ^ --port 8080 ^ --api-key "%LIMEAIAPI_KEY%" ^ --n-gpu-layers 99 ^ --threads 6 ^ --threads-batch 6 ^ --ctx-size %LIMEAICTX% ^ --n-cpu-moe 37 ^ --batch-size 2048 ^ --ubatch-size 1024 ^ --flash-attn on ^ --cache-type-k q8_0 ^ --cache-type-v q8_0 ^ --cache-type-k-draft q8_0 ^ --cache-type-v-draft q8_0 ^ --spec-type draft-mtp ^ --spec-draft-n-max 3 ^ --kv-offload ^ --jinja --chat-template-file chat_template.jinja ^ --mlock ^ --parallel 1 ^ --metrics ^ --no-mmap ^ --cont-batching Lets go over them one by one.1st I set the variables so I can quickly change the model , context and apikey.These are where the llama-serve will serve the openAI compatible API.This is where you set your api key This is how many layers you want in the gpu , I have 99 cause it's "impossible" , forcing to trigger the --fit , you can just use --fit if you want ( --fit-target tells in Mb how much VRAM to leave to the system )as an example : "-fit on -fitt 1024" means "auto-fit everything across devices, but always leave at least 1024 MiB free on each."They control the CPU thread count for two different phases of inference.--threads-batch — threads used during prompt processing (also called prefill or batch processing).--threads — threads used during token generation (decode phase) — the actual one-token-at-a-time autoregressive loop. This phase is heavily memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound.These are where the llama-serve will serve the openAI compatible API.This is where you set your api key This is how many layers you want in the gpu , I have 99 cause it's "impossible" , forcing to trigger the --fit , you can just use --fit if you want ( --fit-target tells in Mb how much VRAM to leave to the system )as an example : "-fit on -fitt 1024" means "auto-fit everything across devices, but always leave at least 1024 MiB free on each."
This is the |Context size , in that example it's 128k (131072) adjust it to use more or less VRAMThis tells his how many experts go to the cpu , adjust this to use more or less VRAM. The more experts in the cpu "the better" , but they will use more VRAM ( too much you will just error out ).Batch size is the max number of tokens processed together during prompt ingestion ubatch size is the physical sub-batch size actually sent to the GPU per step. Bigger values speed up prompt processing but use more VRAM/compute buffer , but they give more SPEEDDDD
Enables Flash Attention, a faster and more memory-efficient way to compute attention. Required if you want to use quantized KV cache without a speed penalty
Sets the Kv Cache format for the Key and Value cache respectively (f16, q8_0, q4_0, etc.).
Same idea as before, but for the Draft model own Kv Cache when using speculative decoding (MTP or a separate draft model)
This turns on MTP Draft and sets it to try to "guess" 3 tokens in the future.
Controls whether the Kv Cache lives in GPU VRAM alongside the model layers, or CPU/system RAM. (Enabled by default)
The KV cache stays on-GPU for speed, since GPU-Local access avoids shuttling data across PCIe on every token.This flag tells the CLI to output or use the model’s chat template in raw Jinja format, instead of the processed/default prompt format. So it can use tools
Note: Qwen models usually improve by using this : https://huggingface.co/froggeric/Qwen-Fixed-Chat-Templates/tree/main downloading the chat_template.jinja , and using --jinja --chat-template-file chat_template.jinja ^Locks the model into physical RAM, preventing the OS from swapping it to disk. Avoids stutters from page faults, but requires you to actually have enough free RAM to hold it locked.Number of concurrent request "slots" the server can handle at once. 1 means single-user, sequential requests only
Raise it if you want to serve multiple simultaneous conversations, at the cost of splitting your KV cache budget across slots.Exposes a Prometheus-compatible metrics endpoint on the server for monitoring things like token throughput and request stats over time.Disables memory-mapped model loading. Normally mmap lets the OS lazily page in the model file, which speeds up load and reduces peak RAM use, disabling it forces the whole model to load into RAM upfront, slower to start but can avoid page-fault stutter mid-inference on some systems.Enables continuous batching : the server can dynamically mix new incoming requests into an in-progress generation batch instead of waiting for the current batch to finish, improving throughput when serving multiple requests. ( actually irrelevant in this config since parallel 1 )
Enables ngram based speculative decoding instead of a model-based drafter (like MTP Draft or a separate draft model). Instead of running any neural network to guess upcoming tokens, it pattern-matches against recent contextSets the maximum n-gram size (lookback window) used for pattern matching to 64.
You can use this and MTP Draft at the same time, but sometimes it introduces slowdowns.
Setting the vision model , so your model can "SEE" images

5-performance/sampling-parameters.html.title: Sampling Parameters
5-performance/sampling-parameters.html.description: Temperature : controls randomness; higher = more varied/creative, lower = more deterministic/focused Top-K : only considers the K most likely next tokens, cutting off the long unlikely tail Top-P (nucleus) : only considers the smallest set of tokens whose combined probability reaches P, adapts dynamically unlike top-K's fixed count Min-P : discards tokens below a probability threshold relative to the top token, another dynamic alternative to top-K Repeat penalty : reduces the probability of tokens the model has already used recently, discourages loops/repetition Presence penalty : flat penalty applied once to any token that has appeared at all in the output so far, encourages topic diversity Frequency penalty : like presence penalty but scales with how many times a token has appeared, stronger discouragement the more it repeats Reasoning budget : (specific to a few models) caps how many tokens the model can spend on internal reasoning before it must produce a final answer Remember lots of times the Harness will send the parameters in the prompt, setting these in llama.cpp is just so it defaults to itYou don't want high temp for coding , it will just be "too creative" and make up stuff, but you also don't want it to be super low temp so it's super deterministic if you give it a more creative task.Here's a few lists "semi well made" to give some good examples, they should make some sense:

9-downloads-and-websites/list-of-online-.title: List of Online Models with simple chat
9-downloads-and-websites/list-of-online-.description:
I do recommend setting up an Harness and your own models , but even to follow this guide , using these will help you with issues and extra information, or even to fact check me.
Claude - Seems to have the best models in my opinion (sonnet,opus and now fable), very tecnical, probably the best one to help following this broken guide.
Deepseek - Chinese made , also very decent , technical, has some problems with "overthinking" and sometimes resets to speaking chinese.
Kimi - chinese made , decent model , quite unknown , technical , good agent use
Z.ai - decent models , especially GLM5.2 and 5.5 , technical .
ChatGPT - "ok" model , very "constrained" , has to be nice , has to avoid "hard subjects" , very biased model when you ask it about specific themes.
copilot - it's GPT model but with little limits ( while chatgpt eventually downgrades you )
Grok - good for "reading the room" in social networks.
Gemini - Decent model , can handle a LOT of context , good UI control over things.
DuckDuckAI - Gives you some free models ( even claude opus ) , but very limited with rates.
Mistral - Le frenchie AI , European , haven't tested it much.
LmArena - lets you "pit" AI vs AI , also can be used to get some answers ;) To have a general talk , or ask random questions , I would probably state Gemini ( cause of finer control ) , GPT and grok would be the best ones.To for example following this guide or asking for tips I would probably rate Claude > Deepseek=Kimi=GLM5.5 ... unsure where I would place GLM5.5 since I didnt do much testing , it might beat deepseek
